# RoleModel Way of Estimating

**Not all estimates have the same level of certainty.** The intention of a ramped scale is that larger estimates are considered much less certain. This is why we say the 64-point card uses 80 hours of effort to compute the dollar amount.

## Point Conversion Chart

| Points | Time | T-Shirt Size |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | < 1 hour | |
| 2 | < 2 hours | X-Small |
| 4 | ~4 hours (< half day) | Small |
| 8 | ~8 hours (~day) | Medium |
| 16 | ~2 days (~20 hours) | Large |
| 32 | ~1 week (~40 hours) | X-Large |
| 64 | ~2 weeks / 1 iteration (~80 hours) | XX-Large |

---

## Key Principles

**Estimates are not contracts.** An estimate is not expected to be a fixed thing that can't be updated. It is instead our best guess of the size of the effort given our current context. Some estimates will stay the same over time, but others will need to be re-validated as we get closer to doing the work. This is also the case when we break down an estimate into its parts — it may be bigger or smaller than the initial estimate on the Epic.

**Estimates should be quick.** Estimates should be cheap and based on an initial understanding of the problem and the system. We are generally trying to gauge the order of magnitude, not the specific number of hours.

**Estimates are needed for partners to make tradeoffs.** Without even a rough estimate on a card, it is nearly impossible for a partner to judge if something is worth doing or if they should be pushing back on finding a smaller way to accomplish the business goal.

**Larger cards carry more uncertainty.** A 64-point card is not just "a lot of work" — it's work where the unknowns are stacked. If a card is sizing at 32 or 64 points, that's a strong signal to explore whether it can be broken into smaller, more predictable pieces.
